He himself, like the Spirit of which he is born, is free and is mysterious in his ways, but discerned by the sound of his works and life. Take the text in reference to THE HOLY SPIRIT HIMSELF. The figure is the wind and, as most of you know, the Hebrew word for, "wind," and for, "spirit," is the same. And it is interesting to note that the same is true with the Greek word, "pneuma," which signifies both, "breath," and, "spirit," so that the figure which the Savior used might very naturally grow out of the word which He employed. The wind is air in motion and is, of course, material. But air is apparently more spiritual than any of the other elements, except fire, since it is not to be grasped by the hand nor seen with the eyes. It is certain that wind really exists, for we hear the sound of it and observe its various effects, but it is not to be touched, handled, or gazed upon. Men cannot traffic in it, or measure it in scales, or weigh it in balances. We may watch the clouds for hours as they hasten along like winged birds, but the wind which drives them is out of

QUESTION:
What reason does Spurgeon give for the Holy Spirit being likened to wind, based on the Hebrew and Greek terms?

My dear friends, the blessed Apostle, in his divine wisdom, points out that the very Hebrew word for "wind" and "spirit" is one and the same, and likewise the Greek "pneuma" embraces both "breath" and "spirit." Thus the Savior, in His perfect illustration, drew upon the very language of the Scriptures, that the mystery of the Holy Ghost might be conveyed as the invisible wind—air in motion—yet ever present and efficacious.

They are not like the holy angels, for they desire to look into these things. Dr. John Owen was, perhaps, the most profound Divine who ever lived, yet Dr. John Owen could not know, on earth, as much about certain things as angels did! QUESTION:
Why does Spurgeon say that the Lord must hate evil even in His most favored creatures?

Were He to pardon sin without a penalty, the foundation of divine law would be shaken, and the order for which He hath such deep concern would be rendered void.

QUESTION:
What does Spurgeon mean when he declares that the death of our Lord was the vindication of God's righteous Law?

He means that Christ's crucifixion was not merely the consummation of obedience, but the triumphant affirmation that God's holy Law stands unbroken; by dying He satisfied the demand of justice, thereby upholding the very law which undergirds all society.

QUESTION:
How does Spurgeon describe our fellowship with Christ in times of sorrow?

He declares that when we have known sorrow, we have walked hand‑in‑hand with our Saviour in affliction. As He wept for the loss of a friend, so too we weep; as He mourned the hardness of men's hearts, so too we mourn; and as He was exceedingly sorry when the hopeful youth turned from Him, so likewise we know that sorrow, for we share in the very broken heart of our Lord.

The Bible, besides all its other excellences, is a great and universal Prayer Book, and has in it petitions suited to all classes and conditions of men at all times, whatever their desires and necessities may be. Now I take out of this Prayer Book this one short supplication. I know the children of God will join with me in praying it, and I trust that before we have done, some who never prayed before may make this their firm prayer, "Show Your marvelous loving kindness." Now, in the first place, we may offer up this prayer-- DESIRING THAT GOD WOULD SHOW MARVELOUS LOVING KINDNESS IN OUR MEDITATIONS. What marvelous loving kindness there is for us to look at! Old as the everlasting hills--but old as it is, and majestic as it must be--there are some eyes that never saw it! Others, too, who, though they have read their Bibles and heard Gospel sermons from their infancy, have never yet seen God's marvelous loving kindness! Let us spend, then, a few minutes in meditation, in order that the Lord may hear this prayer and show us His loving kindness while we muse upon it. QUESTION:
What specific prayer does Spurgeon invite the children of God to join in, and what is its purpose?

Brethren, I bid you lift up the short, earnest supplication, “Show Your marvelous loving kindness.”  It is not a idle request, but a fervent petition that the Almighty may pour out His divine mercy upon our very meditations, that we might behold, even in the quiet of our hearts, that ancient, ever‑lasting love which the hills have witnessed.  By this prayer we invite the Lord to make His kindness manifest to those who have never yet beheld it, that they may be drawn into the glorious light of His grace.

It is so with regard to the house in which we were reared. That old roof tree, that ancient homestead--it may have been covered with thatch and have been one of a group of poor cottages, but still it was our own home and a thousand kindly thoughts gather around the fireside where we, in childhood, nestled beneath a parent's wing. "Our own!" Why, all our relatives are endeared to us by the fact that they are our own! "Father" is a silver word at all times. But, "our father," "our own father"--how the name grows richer and turns to a golden word! "Our own child." "Our own brother." "Our own husband." "Our own wife"--the words are most melodious. We even feel the Bible to be all the dearer to us because we can speak of it as "our own old English Bible." As the Jew's book, coming from God in Hebrew. As a book for the Greek, coming in its latter half to the Gentile in the Greek tongue, it was a priceless treasure. QUESTION:
Why does the passage say that Jesus was glad to be surrounded by unsophisticated, childlike natures rather than by Pharisees and scribes?

He rejoiced, dear brethren, because the Father had poured out His Light upon those lowly and humble souls, whose hearts were untainted by the pretensions and old habits of the learned. In the simple, childlike faith of rustics, fishermen, and the poor, the Gospel could take root anew, unblemished by the proud and hypocritical airs of the Pharisees and scribes, even if they had been converted.

I do not know how it is that we have been kept together in love, helped to abound in labor, and enabled to be firm in the faith, unless it be that special grace has watched over us. We are full of faults; we have nothing to boast of; and yet no church has been more divinely favored: I wonder that the blessing should have lasted so long, and I cannot make it out except when I fall into "supposing him to be the gardener." I cannot trace our prosperity to the pastor, certainly; nor even to my beloved friends the elders and deacons, nor even to the best of you with your fervent love and holy zeal. I think it must be that Jesus has been the gardener, and he has shut the gate when I am afraid I have left it open; and he has driven out the wild boar of the wood just when he had entered to root up the weaker plants. He must have been about at nights to keep off the prowling thieves, and he must have been here, too, in the noontide heat to guard those of you who have prospered in worldly goods, from the glare of too bright a sun. QUESTION:
What image does Spurgeon employ to depict Christ’s protective care over the congregation, and how does he describe the specific dangers He guards against?

My dear saints, I liken our Savior to a vigilant gardener who shuts the gate when I, in trembling fear, would leave it ajar; He drives forth the wild boar that prowls the wood as He roots up the weaker plants; He keeps watch at night to ward off prowling thieves, and in the noontide heat He shields those blessed with worldly goods from the glare of an over‑bright sun. Thus He is ever present to guard us from every peril, both visible and unseen.
